detailedExemplarData.title: Quenching Thirst: A Mathematical Investigation into South Africaâs Water Crisis

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear overall narrative with identifiable introduction, methodology sections and conclusion
Well-structured with headings and short explanations guiding the reader
Relevant content that directly addresses water insecurity in Berg
weaknesses: Abrupt transitions between major sections (e.g. Voronoi to tree-diagram)
Occasional disordered placement of images and page numbering issues
Repetition of basic definitions and lengthy raw data tables reduce conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report demonstrates clear logical development (Band 3) and a conventional structure (Band 3), but conciseness suffers from repeated explanations and verbatim tables (Band 2). On balance, this warrants a level 3 score out of 4 for Presentation.

strengths: Variables are defined and symbols used in math mode in key formulae
Effective use of multiple representations (maps, Voronoi diagram, graphs, matrices)
Most diagrams and tables are interpreted in context
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation for matrices and vectors; typographical noise interferes
Some formulae are in prose rather than symbolic form
Missing explicit display of important equations (e.g. Penman formula)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the report uses a good variety of representations (Band 3), inconsistent notation and occasional clarity issues limit the mathematical communication to Band 2 overall, meriting 2 out of 4.

strengths: Personal stake clearly motivating the choice of Berg and the overall investigation
Student-driven questions shaped the methodology and tool selection
Creative combination of statistics, geometry and graph theory beyond a textbook approach
weaknesses: Methods remain largely standard and not deeply adapted to the studentâs own context
Engagement is significant but not at an exceptionally novel or technically advanced level
Some borrowed elements (e.g. Penman formula) lack deeper personal reinterpretation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands show clear evidence of initiative, personal approach and creativity (Band 2) but not the outstanding originality needed for Band 3. A score of 2 out of 3 is therefore justified.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on limitations of rainfall midpoint and reservoir cost assumptions
Critical comments link model results back to the core question of water insecurity
Proposed extensions (e.g. weighted Voronoi, failure scenario simulations) indicate forward-looking reflection
weaknesses: Reflections, though present, do not probe deeply enough into data variability or methodological assumptions
Critical analysis is meaningful but not sustained at a high level throughout
Connections to enhanced understanding are clear but lack a fully evaluative narrative
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection throughout is meaningful (Band 2) with some critique, but it falls short of the substantial critical reflection and deep analysis of Band 3. Thus, 2 out of 3 is appropriate.

strengths: Use of course-level mathematics (t-test, Voronoi, graph theory, pay-back analysis) is well justified and relevant
Correct implementation of key procedures (e.g. t-test critical value comparison, adjacency-matrix powers)
Creative mathematical modelling (Voronoi for resource allocation, weight-adjusted matrices) demonstrates conceptual understanding
weaknesses: Significant numerical and unit conversion errors (rainfall to mm/s) persist
Justification for weighting one-step vs two-step connections is not mathematically explained
Certain sophisticated arguments (e.g. optimal geometry, NPV analysis) are suggested but not fully developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics used is at course level and generally appropriate (Band 3), but partial correctness and local errors limit full rigor (Band 2). On balance an overall Band 3 level is justified, assigning 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing a personally relevant context (family home in Berg) and deciding which mathematical tools to apply shows significant independent thought, though the methods themselves are standard.  The strand fits the âsignificant evidenceâ band rather than âoutstandingâ.

Across the exploration the student shows sound conceptual grasp of statistical testing, perpendicular bisectors, adjacency matrices and financial rate of return.  Misunderstandings are local rather than systemic, so knowledge is good in parts but not rigorous enough for higher bands.

Calculations are correct in parts (t-value, degrees of freedom) but significant numerical and unit errors appear in the rainfall-per-second conversion and in matrix labelling, indicating only partial correctness.  Thus the work fits âmathematics is partially correctâ.

While most content is relevant to diagnosing water insecurity, the report contains repeated explanations of basic concepts (e.g. definitions of nodes, sites, reservoirs) and very lengthy verbatim data tables.  At 21 pages the discussion could be tighter; hence relevance is good but conciseness only moderate.

Variables are normally defined (e.g. $T_m$, $E$) and symbols appear in maths-mode, yet notation for matrices and vectors is inconsistent and subscripts/superscripts are sometimes lost in the text dump.  Some formulae are written in prose rather than symbolic form, so usage is appropriate in places but not throughout.

Explanation of steps is usually clear, but occasional typographical noise (overlines, misplaced bold figures) reduces readability and minor inconsistencies (mm per s written as mm/s without justification) interrupt coherence.  Hence communication is mostly rather than fully consistent.

All mathematics (t-test, Voronoi, graph theory, Penman equation, pay-back analysis) is squarely within the SL AI syllabus and relevant to the context.  The variety shows purposeful selection but methods remain at standard course level, so ârelevant mathematics at course level usedâ is appropriate.

The student employs a variety of representations: thematic drought map, Voronoi diagram, Desmos graph, large data tables and several adjacency matrices.  Most are integrated into the argument and interpreted in context, meeting the descriptor for âeffective useâ though not yet âsophisticatedâ.

The student comments on limitations of midpoint rainfall, population growth assumptions, and reservoir cost factors, showing meaningful reflection scattered through the text.  Reflection is present in each major section but seldom probes issues deeply enough for the top band.

Combining statistics, Voronoi geometry, graph theory and finance illustrates creativity and initiative beyond a textbook treatment.  The work is inventive but not exceptionally novel or technically advanced, fitting the middle band.

The exploration follows a recognisable narrative: introduction-data selection-statistical test-geometric modelling-financial comparison-conclusion.  Ideas generally follow one another in a sensible order and the reader can trace how each section addresses the central question.  Nevertheless, several transitions are abrupt (e.g. sudden shift from Voronoi to tree-diagram without an explicit link) and some arguments rely on implicit assumptions, so the logical thread is clear rather than exemplary.

Some critical analysis is evident when the student weights one-step paths more heavily than two-step or compares evaporation to rainfall, yet errors (e.g. unit mis-conversion) are not examined, limiting the depth of critique.  Overall, analysis is meaningful but not robust.

Reflective comments routinely link findings back to the central question of water insecurity, explaining how each model influences understanding.  These links are clear but not extended into a strong evaluative narrative, corresponding to band 2.

A conventional structure (title page, introduction, methodology sections, conclusion, bibliography) is evident and headings guide the reader.  Each major part begins with a short explanation of purpose.  However, page numbering, matrix screenshots and image placement are occasionally disordered, so the flow is not seamless enough for the top band.

The investigation is clearly driven by the studentâs own questions (selecting focus area, weighing financial options), demonstrating a personal approach that shapes the work.  However, elements such as the Penman formula are borrowed without deep adaptation, so engagement stops short of highly personal insight.

annotations.comment: The transformation into Desmos and tree diagram shows significant independent initiative in applying graph theory to spatial data.
Matrix A uses overlines and boldface inconsistently. Adopt standard $$A_{ij}=0,1$$ notation and define indices explicitly.
Raising the adjacency matrix to the second power to detect two-step paths demonstrates strong understanding of graph-theoretic methods.
The combined matrix X equally weights oneâstep and twoâstep routes without justification. Consider weighting factors or a probabilistic model.
The studentâs vivid personal connection to South Africa and articulation of motivations demonstrates genuine engagement and a clear personal stake in the topic.
Transition from the introduction to selecting a focus region is abrupt. Add a linking rationale explaining how national data led to the choice of Berg.
Figure 1 is wellâchosen but the caption could be more descriptive. Explicitly interpret the color key and how the circled regions were identified.
Table 1 occupies a full page of raw data. Summarize key values in text or a chart rather than presenting the entire table to improve conciseness.
The criteria for selecting Berg based on yield vs. requirement are sound, but the interpretation of negative balances in parentheses should be explained mathematically.
Introduce a brief transition explaining why the data reduction to three regions naturally leads to the planned statistical tests.
In Table 2 the notation for ranges and midpoints mixes prose and symbols. Use consistent mathematical notation (e.g. $$m_i=\tfrac{a+b}{2}$$) for clarity.
When outlining the paired T-test, state the assumption of normality and comment on sample size adequacy to strengthen mathematical rigor.
The GDC procedure is clearly described, and the comparison of observed and critical $$t$$ values is correctly implemented.
The note on potential skew from an unusually wet 2015 is valid. Deepen this reflection by examining historical rainfall variability.
Applying a Voronoi diagram to model distribution is a creative use of geometry to address real-world water allocation.
The description of midpoint and perpendicular bisector calculation lacks concise symbolic notation. Present the steps with equations, e.g. $$M=\bigl(\frac{x_1+x_2}{2},\frac{y_1+y_2}{2}\bigr)$$.
Acknowledging population size limitations in the Voronoi is excellent; propose using a weighted Voronoi to model differential demand.
Adjusting matrix weights in Y to prioritize direct connections is insightful. Comment on how this choice reflects risk assessment.
Suggestion: simulate failure scenarios on the network and reflect on how matrix Y trends change under different node removals.
Introduce the formula for optimal surface-area-to-depth ratio using calculus or geometric arguments to strengthen mathematical justification.
The Penman evaporation equation is referenced but not explicitly presented. Display the full equation in LaTeX, e.g. $$E=\frac{700(T_m)}{100-A}+15(T-T_d)(80-T)$$.
The evaporation rate calculation compresses unit conversions. Show intermediate steps converting annual rainfall to mm/s to ensure correctness.
Considering net present value or discount rate in the financial analysis would provide a more robust long-term investment evaluation than simple payback.
The conclusion effectively restates methods but could be more concise by focusing on key results and implications for Berg.
Extending the study to multiple regions, as proposed, shows strong critical reflection and vision for broader application.
